title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Across countries in 2008, the share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student varies widely relative to national income levels. The scatter plot places government expenditure per student as a percentage of GDP per capita on the vertical axis, ranging from 0% to over 40%, against GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis spanning from roughly $2,000 to $100,000. Points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania.

Serbia sits at the highest point on the vertical axis, exceeding 40%, while Kazakhstan rests near the bottom at roughly 1%. Djibouti, Albania, and Ukraine cluster between 30% and 35%. In the middle range, countries like Cyprus, South Korea, and Latvia hover around 25% to 30%. A dense grouping of European nations, including Austria, Malta, and Estonia, occupies the upper-right quadrant where GDP per capita exceeds $50,000 and spending shares sit between 20% and 25%. Conversely, many African nations such as Niger, Burundi, and DR Congo appear in the lower-left, with GDP per capita below $2,000 and spending shares between 5% and 15%. Asian countries show significant spread; India and Cambodia sit near 10%, while Thailand and Bhutan reach higher shares around 15% to 20%. North American and Oceanian points, including the United States and Australia, generally align with high GDP per capita and moderate spending shares around 20%.

The distribution shows no single linear relationship between national wealth and the relative share of income dedicated to primary education, as high-spending nations appear across both low and high income brackets.
